forecasts.reasoning:
The Artemis Accords have sustained a strong momentum of accessions since 2023, with approximately 11 of the last 17 months seeing at least one new signatory (a ~65% base rate). Over the past six months alone, multiple new nations have joined, including Bangladesh in April 2025 and Norway in May 2025. 

Furthermore, the month of June provides excellent venues for space diplomacy that tend to cluster such announcements. The UN Committee on the Peaceful Uses of Outer Space (COPUOS) holds its 68th session in Vienna starting June 25, 2025. Historically, the start of COPUOS corresponds with major diplomatic pronouncements. In addition, the Paris Air Show takes place in mid-June 2025, which is another frequent anchor for aerospace and space agreements. Although many of the primary U.S. space allies have already signed (leaving slightly less "low-hanging fruit" among the remaining nations), discussions are known to be active with others, such as Portugal, which formally signaled intent a year prior. 

The primary risks for a NO resolution are random Poisson variance (simply no country finalizing their paperwork in time for June), or a COPUOS signing being delayed to the July 1-4 portion of the conference schedule, missing the June 30 deadline. Adjusting the strong ~65% base rate upward to account for the major diplomatic events, a 74% probability optimally straddles the robust historical trend and the event-driven catalysts without assuming absolute certainty.

The base rate for a sitting U.S. Senator resigning or switching caucuses within a 1-month period (from late May to July 1) is exceptionally low. Although Senator Fetterman has faced intense scrutiny, progressive calls for his resignation over his health and ideological stances, and reports of growing isolation (such as leaving a Democratic caucus group chat), he has defiantly refused to resign—explicitly stating so on May 8, 2025. Additionally, he previously shot down rumors of a party switch in January 2025. Congress members have not echoed the calls for his resignation, and both allies and detractors predict he will not step down soon. Defiant lawmakers rarely resign abruptly without a formal push from leadership or a decisive, acute health collapse. Given there are only 32 days remaining until the resolution date and no current developments indicate a formal caucus exit or resignation is imminent, the probability is extremely low.

The Artemis Accords have acquired 55 signatories in roughly 55 months, yielding a base rate of roughly 1 new signatory per month on average. However, signings are not uniformly distributed; they heavily cluster around diplomatic summits and major international space conferences. Specifically, historically at least one country has signed the Accords every single June since their creation in 2020 (Brazil in June 2021, France in June 2022, India and Ecuador in June 2023, Armenia in June 2024). A key driver for this seasonality is the main regular session of the UN Committee on the Peaceful Uses of Outer Space (COPUOS), which typically takes place in June. The 68th session of COPUOS is scheduled to take place at the UN in Vienna from June 25 to July 4, 2025. Given the perfect historical track record of June signings, the ongoing momentum of the Accords, and the well-timed COPUOS session acting as a catalyst for formalized announcements, there is a strong structural likelihood that at least one new country will sign in June 2025. I assign a 73% probability.

The base rate for a sitting U.S. Senator resigning or switching caucuses within a ~30-day window is historically very low (<< 1%). Fetterman is currently facing intense scrutiny following an early May 2025 New York Magazine profile alleging erratic behavior, which has prompted progressive groups in Pennsylvania to call for his resignation. He also reportedly left the Senate Democratic Caucus group chat. 

However, Fetterman has forcefully and repeatedly denied these allegations, dismissed the profile as a "hit piece," and vowed not to resign. Earlier in January 2025, he also robustly shut down rumors of a party switch or becoming an Independent (explicitly stating he would not make a "Manchin or Sinema play"). Furthermore, Democratic Senate leadership like Chuck Schumer and Dick Durbin continue to express support and work with him, effectively ruling out any forced expulsion from the caucus. Since Fetterman has already weathered the peak of the initial controversy for nearly a month without wavering, the chances of him suddenly resigning, switching, or being removed from the caucus in the remaining 33 days before July 1 are extremely low. The probability rests almost entirely on an unforeseeable severe health emergency occurring in the next month.
